data.title: Palladyne AI reiterates $24M–$27M 2026 revenue target as backlog rises to $18M following structural transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Palladyne AI Corp. (PDYN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Benjamin Wolff stated that "we are reiterating 2026 revenue guidance of $24 million to $27 million. That is roughly 4 to 5x our 2025 revenue." He highlighted that the company’s backlog has increased from approximately $13.5 million at the end of 2025 to nearly $18 million midway through the first quarter. Wolff emphasized the completion of a "structural transformation" in November, moving the company from a development-stage AI business to a "vertically integrated embodied AI-centric industrial and defense platform company generating meaningful revenues."
  • Wolff described the release of Palladyne IQ 2.0 and the signing of the company’s first commercial IQ customer contract, noting this deal as "strategically important." He also announced the integration of SwarmOS into the BRAIN avionics platform, demonstrations of autonomous drone swarming, and advancements in the Gremlin-X mini-bomber drone.
  • The company expanded its relationship with Portal Space Systems and recently secured a contract for a missile propulsion subsystem from a major defense prime customer.
  • CFO Trevor Thatcher reported, “Revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 increased 118% to $1.7 million compared to $0.8 million last year. The increase was due to the inclusion of post-acquisition revenues from the acquired companies.” Thatcher said, “GAAP net loss for the fourth quarter was $1.5 million or $0.04 per share.” He added that the company finished 2025 with approximately $47 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities.

Outlook

  • The company reiterated its 2026 revenue guidance of $24 million to $27 million, reflecting both the contribution of acquired businesses and expected organic growth. Thatcher stated, “We currently expect 2026 consolidated quarterly operating cash usage of approximately $8 million to $9 million.” Management expects ongoing investment in SwarmOS, IQ, and newly acquired programs, with further headcount increases to support growth.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q4 2025 rose to $1.7 million, with cost of revenue at $1.4 million. Research and development expense was $3.8 million, general and administrative expense $4.7 million, and sales and marketing expense $1 million. Operating loss for the quarter was $9.3 million. Non-GAAP net loss stood at $6.9 million or $0.16 per share. The company reported a $4.6 million noncash gain related to warrant liabilities and a $2.5 million income tax benefit tied to a November acquisition. The Q4 net cash burn rate was approximately $10 million, including acquisition and debt paydown.

Q&A

  • Greg Konrad, Jefferies LLC: Asked about 2026 growth drivers in M&A versus organic business. Wolff responded the company expects growth across manufacturing, UAV, and AI but is "not giving any specific guidance on the specific growth in those 3 categories."
  • Konrad: Queried key contracts and growth drivers for 2026. Wolff said the defense side offers "multiple shots on goal," highlighting aggressive pursuit of collaborative autonomy and swarming contracts, as well as continued growth in the AI business and commercial sector.
  • Konrad: Inquired about missile contract visibility. Wolff explained, "We can soup to nuts be participating in that missile process," and stressed their role in both new and existing missile programs.
  • Konrad: Asked for clarification on cash usage. Thatcher confirmed, "That's our expected cash used in operations."
  • Konrad: Asked about CapEx needs. Thatcher said, "I wouldn't say they're significant right now...we don't see any real significant CapEx needs."
  • Konrad: Questioned profitability and gross margin outlook. Thatcher withheld guidance below the revenue line, while Wolff added, "We're focusing on those higher margin opportunities so that we keep our margins across the entire enterprise relatively robust."
  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about Red Cat and Draganfly OEM partnerships. Thatcher said, "We expect to be certified on Red Cat drones virtually any time," and implementation with Draganfly is expected this quarter.
  • Kinstlinger: Probed timing for IQ and Pilot production units. Wolff said, "The first sale of IQ 2.0 is a production deployment...in the coming weeks."
  • Kinstlinger: Asked about customer education. Wolff detailed ongoing demonstrations and outreach.
  • Kinstlinger: Asked about impacts from conflict in Iran. Wolff said, "I haven't seen any change other than additional inquiries and interest levels."
  • Kinstlinger: Requested R&D priorities. Wolff listed "material advancement on our 2 UAV platforms, the Gremlin-X and the SwarmStrike," and continued enhancement of SwarmOS and IQ.
  • Mike Latimore, Northland Capital Markets: Asked about the backlog surge. Wolff declined detailed breakdown but cited momentum across all units. Latimore also inquired about the value of integration, pricing of SwarmOS, and the range of drones targeted—Wolff described a per-drone licensing model and broad applicability.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on revenue composition, growth drivers, contract visibility, and partnership progress, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Analysts pressed for specifics on profitability, backlog composition, and OEM timelines but received measured responses.
  • Management emphasized optimism and confidence in the company’s strategic direction, using phrases such as "we are very optimistic" and "I think we have a lot of tailwinds for us on that side of the business." During Q&A, management remained consistent with prepared remarks, often reiterating the focus on execution and the phased "crawl, walk, run" roadmap.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more confident, specifically around backlog growth and operational execution. Analyst tone remained consistent, with recurring questions about financial breakdowns and operational milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reiterated and quantified 2026 revenue guidance for the first time, now at $24 million to $27 million, compared to the previous quarter’s statement that revenue was expected to "more than triple" from about $8 million in 2024.
  • The backlog grew from over $10 million in Q3 to nearly $18 million by mid-Q1 2026, signaling increased contract momentum.
  • Management provided greater detail on operational execution, product deployments, customer contracts, and integration of acquisitions than in the previous quarter.
  • Analyst focus shifted from the rationale for acquisitions and integration benefits in Q3 to concrete execution, backlog conversion, and growth drivers in Q4.
  • Management’s confidence appeared strengthened, repeatedly referencing momentum and the benefits of the new business structure.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged risks around executing development programs, backlog conversion, scaling production, integrating acquisitions, securing contracts, maintaining liquidity, and adapting to shifting defense and commercial markets.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the timing and magnitude of contract wins, the ramp-up in cash usage, the lack of detailed margin guidance, and progress with key OEM partners.
  • Management’s mitigation approach centers on focused investment in R&D, operational integration, and leveraging multiple business units for diversified revenue opportunities.

Final Takeaway

Palladyne AI Corp. enters 2026 as a structurally transformed, vertically integrated defense and industrial AI platform company, reiterating revenue guidance of $24 million to $27 million and reporting a growing backlog reaching nearly $18 million. The company is focused on delivering commercial and defense product milestones, scaling contract wins across multiple domains, and executing against a defined roadmap that emphasizes both organic and acquired growth, with the aim of converting strategic transformation into measurable financial performance in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: From oil shock to recession? History says it depends on the Fed
data.description:
Decades of oil shocks offer clues to how markets behave when geopolitical tensions hit energy flows.

Societe Generale analyst Manish Kabra's assessment of five major geopolitical oil shocks over the past half century shows that risk aversion in financial markets typically persists well beyond the first week of the crisis, while crude prices (CL1:COM) (USO) tend to reach their peak roughly three months after the initial disruption.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) (GLD) and the U.S. dollar (DXY) have historically provided the strongest protection during such episodes, while U.S. equities broadly outpace global peers, Kabra wrote in a note to clients. Emerging-market equities (EEM), meanwhile, often recover more quickly than their European counterparts (IEV) once the initial shock fades.

"Fundamentally, two variables matter: 1) Shock duration; 2) the Fed’s reaction function, 70s Oil shocks driven recession were amplified by Fed tightening. Three of the five shocks triggered a US recession: the last two amid more resilient growth," the analyst said.

Prediction markets are still implying relatively low recession risk this year, as the Fed keeps rates on hold as policymakers wait for clearer evidence that inflation is sustainably returning to target while assessing risks to growth and employment. Traders on Kalshi pricing the probability of a U.S. downturn at about 21%, roughly unchanged since the war in Iran started on Feb. 28 and down from 22.5% a month earlier.

Kabra contended that the most likely oil-price scenario amid the deepening Middle East conflict is a "short-lived spike, then partial retracement." Crude oil prices have surged over 20% since the U.S. and Israel launched coordinated airstrikes on Iran, changing hands at $78.70 per barrel at press time.

For positioning, he said he favors "broadening equity exposures such as S&P equal‑weight (RSPE) (EQWL), small/mid‑caps (RUT) (VO), peripheral Europe, China onshore, energy beneficiaries, Japan trading houses (MUFG), and shipping (BOAT)."

data.title: Bioventus outlines $600M–$610M 2026 sales target with accelerated investment in PNS, PRP, and ultrasonics
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bioventus Inc. (BVS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Claypoole highlighted that "Bioventus delivered another solid quarter and concluded a successful year across our strategic priorities while helping patients recover so they can live life to the fullest." He emphasized that the company has transformed over the past three years, establishing a strong foundation and is now entering a phase focused on accelerating revenue growth, strengthening earnings power, and expanding capital allocation optionality through consistent free cash flow.
  • Claypoole reported 10% organic revenue growth, record quarterly cash from operations at approximately $38 million, and an adjusted EBITDA margin expansion by nearly 500 basis points year-over-year. He also cited positive market feedback from pilot launches of peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) and platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as new growth drivers.
  • The CEO announced a 2026 strategy to allocate approximately $13 million of incremental investment toward PNS, PRP, ultrasonics, and international expansion, stating, "Our PNS platform will receive the largest share of the incremental investment, given the rapidly expanding market, our highly differentiated technology and the enormous potential of this business." He reaffirmed the expectation that PNS and PRP will provide a minimum of 200 basis points of growth in 2026.
  • CFO Mark Singleton stated, "Revenue of $158 million increased 3% compared to the prior year. Organic growth was 10% after adjusting for the impact of our advanced rehabilitation divestiture at the end of 2024, which was a result of strong performance across Pain Treatments and Restorative Therapies."

Outlook

  • Singleton provided 2026 guidance, stating, "We expect net sales to range from $600 million to $610 million." He detailed that adjusted earnings per share are forecasted at $0.73 to $0.77 for the year, and cash from operations is projected between $82 million and $87 million, representing a 10% to 17% increase.
  • Management expects Q1 revenue growth to be below the implied guidance range due to fewer selling days and inventory rebalancing, with acceleration anticipated in Q2 and the second half of 2026 as PRP and PNS investments deliver more meaningful growth.

Financial Results

  • Singleton reported adjusted EBITDA of $37 million for the quarter, $8 million higher than the prior year, representing a 30% increase, and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 23%, up 490 basis points year-over-year.
  • Adjusted earnings were $0.24 per diluted share for Q4, and adjusted gross margin reached 76%, 180 basis points higher than the prior year. Cash flow from operations totaled $38 million, nearly double the previous year’s fourth quarter.
  • The company ended the quarter with $51 million in cash, $294 million in outstanding debt, and a net leverage ratio below 2.5x, with a target to reduce net leverage well below 2x by the end of 2026.

Q&A

  • Chase Knickerbocker, Craig-Hallum: Asked about pain segment growth, price vs. volume contribution, and HA business trends. Claypoole responded that “our business continues to be driven by volume...and saw a good performance across the portfolio,” with DUROLANE as the primary growth driver.
  • Knickerbocker: Inquired about 2026 segment assumptions and guide. Singleton explained, "Restorative Therapies segment to be low to mid-single-digit growth in 2026... mid- to high single-digit growth in the pain portfolio... double-digit growth in [surgical] portfolio."
  • Knickerbocker: Questioned deceleration in HA organic growth for early 2026. Claypoole attributed it to "selling days in Q1 and normalizing inventories, but mainly because of our very intentional approach to continuously play the long game and to go after business that is accretive to our profitable growth."
  • Michael Petusky, Barrington Research: Asked about Q4 distributor dynamics and tailwind quantification. Singleton estimated distributor dynamics contributed about $2 million in Q4 and noted that Q1 growth will be the lowest due to these factors.
  • Petusky: Requested PNS pilot learnings and reaffirmation of the 200 basis point expectation from PNS and PRP. Claypoole confirmed positive feedback on technology and reaffirmed the "minimum of 200 basis points from the contribution of PNS and PRP combined."
  • Caitlin Cronin, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about Ultrasonics expansion and sales rep needs. Claypoole emphasized focus on the spine space, with investment in sales and marketing to expand both spine and neuro.
  • Cronin: Inquired about PNS team build-out cadence. Claypoole confirmed rapid scaling and the hiring of a new General Manager, Megan Rosengarten, to lead the PNS business.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment regarding execution and growth, with multiple congratulations on the quarter’s results. Analysts pressed for segment-level clarity and sustainability of the growth trajectory but did not express skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, especially during prepared remarks, repeatedly citing transformation, strong execution, and confident projections. During Q&A, management provided detailed and direct responses, reaffirming growth targets and investment strategies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, with more aggressive forward-looking statements and larger-scale investment commitments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a shift toward accelerated investment in new growth drivers, with explicit incremental spending planned for PNS, PRP, and Ultrasonics, compared to a stronger focus on margin expansion in the previous quarter.
  • Guidance language evolved to include specific 2026 targets for revenue, EPS, and cash from operations, whereas the previous quarter reiterated 2025 guidance and deferred detailed 2026 projections.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on pain and surgical businesses but showed heightened interest in the scale and timing of new investments this quarter.
  • Management sentiment was more assertive regarding Bioventus’s transformation and growth outlook, and analyst tone shifted positively in response to strong performance and explicit guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management referenced unplanned losses from FX movements, citing "an unplanned loss of almost $1 million" for the quarter and absorption of more than $3 million in FX impacts for the year.
  • Q1 2026 is expected to be the weakest quarter due to fewer selling days and inventory normalization, which could pressure initial results.
  • The company noted continued exposure to tariffs and foreign exchange, though guidance does not assume additional USD fluctuation impacts.
  • Management’s mitigation strategies include improved inventory management, operational efficiencies, and targeting profitability to support investment in growth drivers.

Final Takeaway

Bioventus management highlighted a pivotal transition to accelerated investment in high-potential growth drivers, projecting net sales between $600 million and $610 million for 2026 and aiming for adjusted earnings per share of $0.73 to $0.77. The company plans to leverage its strong core business, record operational cash flow, and expanded adjusted EBITDA margin to fund targeted expansion in PNS, PRP, Ultrasonics, and international markets, while maintaining a disciplined approach to profitability and capital allocation. Management expressed strong confidence in the company’s ability to deliver above-market revenue growth, improved earnings, and enhanced shareholder value in the upcoming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: AMD, PLTR, PM, VST
data.description:
Recent analyst sentiment shifts highlight a growing optimism for high-growth tech firms like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Palantir Technologies (PLTR). Analysts Oliver Rodzianko and Danil Sereda have issued upgrades for these companies, citing strong positions in the AI-driven compute market and robust quarterly revenue growth. In contrast, valuation concerns have triggered more cautious stances on Vistra Corp. (VST) and Philip Morris International (PM), where analysts Blake Winiecki and Bela Lakos suggest elevated multiples and recent price surges mean these stocks are currently fully valued.

Upgrades

  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): Upgrade to Buy by Oliver Rodzianko. The analyst highlights AMD’s compelling value, trading at a forward PEG significantly below sector medians, and its leadership position in the burgeoning AI-driven compute market.

“AMD's management has guided to ~$9.8B in Q1 FY26 revenue, plus or minus $0.3B, which would represent +32% year-over-year growth. This likely sustains present sentiment in the base case or edges shares higher on acknowledgment of the consistency of medium-term growth.”

  • Palantir Technologies (PLTR): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Danil Sereda. Following a significant contraction in its valuation multiple and exceptional Q4 results showing 70% year-over-year revenue growth, the analyst believes the company’s model-agnostic AI platform offers a unique buying opportunity.

“I like the business model's scaling, and I don't see major risks from the recent Anthropic case. The upside might come from PLTR's deeper integration into DoD's workflows and some possible boost from the commercial side of PLTR's business.”

Downgrades

  • Vistra (VST): Downgrade to Hold by Blake Winiecki. While the company has secured long-term nuclear power agreements with tech giants like AWS and Meta, elevated operating costs and an expensive valuation relative to peers warrant a neutral stance.

“The integrated retail electricity and power generation company Vistra Corp. (VST) just released a mixed Q4 earnings report that has led to a 13% drop in its stock price over the last week. … VST’s current PE of 76x and price-to-book of 21x are well above peers, warranting caution until earnings growth and cost control materialize.”

  • Philip Morris International (PM): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Bela Lakos. A rapid 15% price appreciation in just two months has left the stock trading above fair value estimates, according to updated dividend discount models.

“Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) is one of the key global players in the tobacco and smoke-free products space. … My updated dividend discount model, using the latest earnings to evaluate PM’s valuation, suggest that the stock is fully valued, with limited upside potential from the current price point.”

data.title: Xencor falls as royalty dispute with Astra leads to guidance cut
data.description:
Xencor (XNCR) shares dropped ~8% on Thursday after the company lowered its outlook following a royalty dispute related to Ultomiris, a rare disease therapy it has developed in partnership with AstraZeneca (AZN) unit Alexion Pharmaceuticals.

According to Xencor (XNCR), Alexion, which has developed Ultomiris using its Xtend Fc technology, has informed the company that it no longer owes any extra royalties linked to net sales of the drug in the U.S.

While the licensee intends to continue royalty payments linked to Ultomiris sales outside the U.S., Xencor (XNCR) CEO Bassil Dahiyat said that “we plan to work toward a resolution in this matter.”

“We have updated our year-end cash guidance and conservatively adjusted our operating runway estimate into mid-2028.”

The company projected its cash, cash equivalents, and marketable debt securities to reach $380M - $400M by the end of this year, compared to the $400M - $430M it estimated last month. Xencor’s (XNCR) revised cash runway forecast also fell short of the prior guidance, which indicated a runway through 2028.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: AVGO, TTD, CIEN, AEO, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched lower on Thursday as investors eyed the Iran conflict entering day six, with oil prices extending this week's rally amid fears of Middle East supply disruptions.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • The Trade Desk (TTD) +29% - Shares surged following a report that OpenAI held early talks to partner with the firm to help it sell advertising, as it leans on external partners to ramp up its business. According to the report, OpenAI has begun testing ads in ChatGPT in the U.S. for logged-in adult users on the Free and Go tiers and is also exploring partnerships with brands, media agencies, and ad-tech firms as part of an early ads pilot.
  • Okta (OKTA) +10% - Shares climbed after the identity management company issued stronger-than-expected FY2027 subscription revenue guidance and drew positive analyst commentary around its growing traction in agentic identity. The company projected FY2027 subscription revenue growth of about 10% Y/Y, above the 9% consensus estimate, despite a one-point headwind from shifting professional services work to global system integrator partners. Analysts from JPMorgan Chase, Jefferies Financial Group, and RBC Capital Markets reiterated bullish ratings, highlighting early adoption of AI-driven identity solutions across Auth0 and Okta platforms, with expectations that scaling products and partner-led services could support subscription growth acceleration in the coming years.
  • Veeva Systems (VEEV) +7% - Shares advanced after the company's Q4 financial results beat on both lines and issued strong FY2027 revenue and profit guidance. The cloud solutions provider for the life sciences industry sees revenue in FY2027 of $3.585B-$3.6B, above the consensus of $3.56B. Non-GAAP EPS is projected at $8.85, surpassing the consensus of $8.60.
  • Broadcom (AVGO) +4% - Shares rose after the semiconductor and software giant reported FQ1 results and guidance that topped Wall Street estimates. The company expects FQ2 revenue of about $22B with adjusted EBITDA of roughly 68%, well above the $20.5B consensus. Broadcom highlighted strong momentum in AI semiconductor solutions, with Q1 AI revenue surging 106% Y/Y to $8.4B, driven by demand for custom AI accelerators and networking products, and projected AI semiconductor revenue to reach $10.7B in Q2.
  • Cracker Barrel Old Country Store (CBRL) +2% - Shares surged after posting better-than-expected top- and bottom-line results and narrowing its FY2026 outlook. The restaurant chain now expects FY2026 revenue of $3.24B–$3.27B vs. the prior $3.2B–$3.3B range, with the new midpoint of $3.255B slightly above the $3.25B consensus estimate. Adjusted EBITDA is projected at $85M–$100M compared with the earlier $70M–$110M outlook, while commodity inflation is expected at 2%–2.5% vs. the prior 2.5%–3.5%. The company also lowered its planned investment to $105M–$115M from the earlier $110M–$125M range.

Biggest stock losers

  • Ciena (CIEN) -14% - Shares tumbled despite reporting FQ1 results that topped expectations and raising its FY2026 revenue outlook. The networking systems provider posted Q1 revenue of $1.43B, up 33% Y/Y, while adjusted EPS surged 111% to $1.35, both ahead of estimates. CFO Marc Graff highlighted a historically strong order book and record backlog, signaling durable demand into 2027. For FQ2, the company expects revenue of about $1.5B ± $50M with an adjusted operating margin of 17.5%–18.5% and a gross margin of 43.5%–44.5%. Ciena also lifted its FY2026 revenue outlook to $5.9B–$6.3B (midpoint $6.1B), above its prior $5.7B–$6.1B forecast and ahead of the $5.96B consensus estimate.
  • Victoria’s Secret & Co. (VSCO) -14% - Shares plunged despite reporting better-than-expected Q4 results and issuing guidance above estimates, as a decline in unadjusted income and plans to explore a sale of DailyLook along with an impairment charge tied to Adore Me assets weighed on sentiment. CEO Hillary Super said the company delivered an exceptional quarter and year, while CFO and COO Scott Sekella highlighted strong execution and margin performance, with profits rising 22% despite $85M in tariff pressures. However, unadjusted operating income fell 14.5% to $229M, and unadjusted net income slipped 4.7% to $184M, or $2.14 per share. Looking ahead, the company expects Q1 sales of $1.49B–$1.52B, above $1.42B estimates, and FY2026 revenue of $6.85B–$6.95B, exceeding the $6.80B consensus.
  • American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) -12% - Shares declined after the retailer reported a Q4 beat on both revenue and earnings but issued a weaker-than-expected full-year outlook amid tariff-related margin pressure and a 10% rise in year-end inventory. Gross margin declined 30 basis points to 37%, reflecting a 280-basis-point tariff impact that was partly offset by lower costs, favorable currency movements, and operational efficiencies. For the current quarter, the company expects high-single-digit comparable sales growth and operating income of $20M–$25M, above the $15M estimate. For FY2026, comparable sales are projected to rise mid-single digits with operating income of $390M–$410M, while capital expenditures are expected to total $250M–$260M, roughly half of earlier expectations.
  • Rigetti Computing (RGTI) -6% - Shares dipped after the quantum computing firm reported Q4 results that missed Wall Street estimates. Despite the miss, CEO Subodh Kulkarni said the company remains focused on executing its roadmap, including deploying its 108-qubit system with 99.5% median two-qubit gate fidelity and advancing toward larger-scale systems to achieve quantum advantage. Rigetti also expects significant Y/Y revenue growth in Q1, supported by shipments tied to previously announced $5.7M Novera on-premises system purchase orders.

data.title: Most and least shorted small-to-mid-cap stocks
data.description:
Short interest remains highly concentrated among a handful of small-to-mid-cap stocks, with Kaixin Holdings (KXIN), Signing Day Sports (SGN), and Avidity Biosciences (RNA) topping the list, while Regional Health Properties (RHEP), WF International Limited (WXM), and Republic Bancorp (RBCAA) are among the least shorted names.

High short interest can signal bearish sentiment toward a company, but it can also raise the potential for short squeezes if positive catalysts drive share prices higher. In contrast, stocks with very low short interest typically reflect limited bearish positioning or lower trading activity, suggesting investors see fewer near-term downside risks.

Here’s a list of the most shorted small-to-mid-cap stocks:

  • Kaixin Holdings (KXIN) – 82.36%
  • Signing Day Sports (SGN) – 69.75%
  • Avidity Biosciences (RNA) – 65.46%
  • Paranovus Entertainment Technology (PAVS) – 61.67%
  • Greenlane Holdings (GNLN) – 52.17%
  • SCWorx (WORX) – 47.98%
  • KalVista Pharmaceuticals (KALV) – 39.16%
  • Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) – 38.31%
  • Tango Therapeutics (TNGX) – 34.92%
  • Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX) – 33.18%

Least shorted small-to-mid-cap tech stocks (0.50% and above):

  • Regional Health Properties (RHEP) – 0.50%
  • WF International Limited (WXM) – 0.50%
  • Republic Bancorp (RBCAA) – 0.50%
  • AMREP Corporation (AXR) – 0.50%
  • First Bank (FRBA) – 0.51%
  • Texxon Holding (NPT) – 0.51%
  • KNOT Offshore Partners (KNOP) – 0.51%
  • Bridger Aerospace Group Holdings (BAER) – 0.52%
  • Next Technology Holding (NXTT) – 0.52%
  • Education Management Corporation (EDMC) – 0.52%

Here’s a list of small- and mid-cap ETFs: iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR), Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB), iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), Vanguard Mid-Cap ETF (VO), iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), and the SPDR S&P MidCap 400 ETF Trust (MDY).
